Slapp-Happy Companies
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Corporate America complains that lawsuits are crippling productivity. Chrysler is seeking sanctions against lawyers who have filed unsuccessful class-action suits alleging that the company put faulty brakes in cars. A California referendum that would have capped fees for lawyers who represent injury victims was narrowly defeated on Tuesday. Under industry pressure, the Senate passed a tort reform bill limiting damages in lawsuits involving faulty products. 

     One might almost believe that corporations are constant victims of lawsuits.  But they are actually doing much of the suing.

The Beef That Built an Empire
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The outbreak of mad cow disease has struck at the very heart of England's national identity. We British are not only what we eat, but what we believe we eat. And beef has always summoned up images of the red blood and muscle power that created Britannia, ruled the waves and put the fire in John Bull's belly. 

     But now, great haunches of beef revolving on a spit in a castle kitchen are only the stuff of Mel Gibson movies. The reality, for some decades, has been much less imposing: the ubiquitous American-style beefburger, the convenience food par excellence.

A Dangerous Delay in Montana
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Federal authorities are to be commended for finally arresting four members of the armed Freemen group near Jordan, Mont. But the arrests of two of the group's leaders, LeRoy Schweitzer and Daniel Peterson, should have been made months, even years, ago. 

     The Government has been reluctant -- especially after its missteps at Ruby Ridge, Idaho -- to arrest members of the far right who face criminal charges. This timidity, however, has only made the situation worse. Indeed, it may have lead directly to the current standoff between the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the remaining Freemen members, who are bunkered in their ranch, refusing to be arrested for a scheme involving fraudulent money orders and checks.
